chunk_summary: This section addresses culturally sensitive counseling to reduce prelacteal feeds, food taboos, and promote low-cost, nutrient-dense Indian food options for infants and young children aged 0–24 months in India.
learning_objectives: Understand the risks associated with prelacteal feeds and colostrum avoidance.
Learn culturally respectful strategies to address food taboos and promote nutrient-dense diets.
Identify low-cost, locally available Indian food options for infant and young child feeding.
Explore ways to link families to government nutrition programs like PDS and Anganwadi THR.
key_facts: Prelacteal feeds like honey and sugar water increase infection risk and delay milk let-down.
Colostrum is the infant’s first vaccine and should not be avoided.
Breast milk contains sufficient water; early water supplementation increases diarrhea risk.
Low-cost options include khichdi with vegetables, curd with fruit, and groundnut powder in porridge.
Linking families to PDS and Anganwadi THR can improve access to nutrient-dense foods.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Colostrum is the infant’s first vaccine — Indian Pediatrics, Infant and young child feeding guidelines 2016.
document_key_topics: Addressing food taboos in Indian households
Low-cost, nutrient-dense food options for infants
Linking families to government nutrition programs
